summary: Gruber revisits Bloomberg's 2018 'Big Hack' story, which alleged Chinese intelligence compromised Supermicro server motherboards used by Apple, Amazon, and others. He notes that five years later, zero evidence has surfaced to support the claims, and every named company denied the report. Bloomberg never retracted the story. A Mastodon user points out that investing in Supermicro stock after reading Gruber's debunking posts proved highly profitable, with shares rising from $11.65 to $164.56. Gruber treats the stock recovery as a fitting postscript to what he considers a disgraceful episode in journalism.
key_points: Bloomberg's 2018 'Big Hack' story alleged Chinese spy chips on Supermicro motherboards but presented zero confirmable evidence, and none has emerged in five years
Every company named in the report flatly denied it, yet Bloomberg has never issued a retraction
Supermicro's stock plummeted nearly 50% after the story but has since risen to over 14 times that post-crash price
Bloomberg's refusal to retract has permanently tarnished their journalistic credibility in Gruber's view
money_quotes: Bloomberg presented zero actual evidence then, and five years later, zero evidence of such rogue chips has been found.
Every company Bloomberg alleged was compromised flatly denied the report.
Bloomberg has never retracted the report, thus forever tarnishing their journalistic integrity.

I can't believe I never thought to follow-up on their share price, given my years-long zealotry reminding people, via footnotes in other reports from Bloomberg, that 'The Big Hack' was by all available evidence complete bullshit, yet never retracted.
Their stock closed today at $164.56 — over 14 times higher than it was after 'The Big Hack' dropped.
What a nice postscript to a disgraceful story.
key_insight: Bloomberg's refusal to retract an evidence-free story that cratered a company's stock price is a lasting indictment of their journalistic standards, made all the more stark by Supermicro's spectacular recovery.
